name: read_file

systemPrompt: Use this skill to read file contents. For large files exceeding context limits, the skill will return file info and suggest using chunkIndex to read in parts. When a file returns warning='FILE_TOO_LARGE', iterate through chunks with chunkIndex=0, 1, 2... to process the entire file. **Do NOT call read_file on archives** (.zip/.tar/.tgz/.tar.gz/.tar.bz2/.tar.xz/.7z/.rar/.gz/.bz2/.xz) - they're binary, the read will fail with a codec error. name: scan_source_files
description: Batch-read every source file under a directory in one call. Walks recursively, skips .git/node_modules/etc and binary files by extension, caps per-file and total output. Use for repo audits where you need coverage of every file — faster and more thorough than many sequential read_file calls.
systemPrompt: **PRIMARY TOOL for whole-repo audits, malicious-code reviews, or any 'check all files' / 'review everything' / 'find X across the repo' request.** Call this AFTER git_clone_shallow whenever the user's intent is to review the current state of a repo, even if they linked to a specific commit page — cloning at that SHA checks out the working tree and scan_source_files then reads every source file. One call, full coverage of up to 300 files (default caps: 4000 chars/file, 500,000 chars total); recursively walks and auto-excludes .git / node_modules / __pycache__ / .venv / dist / build / Pods / .idea / .vscode / coverage / etc. Prefer this over: (a) multiple sequential read_file calls — you will give up at 3-7 files and miss coverage; (b) git_show_commit — that only shows one commit's diffs, heavily truncated, not the current file state. **IMPORTANT — extension parameters:** DO NOT pass `extensions=[...]` for a security audit. The default extension set is already broad and covers ~50 languages plus platform config. Passing a narrow list like `['py','js','ts']` EXCLUDES critical files like .pbxproj, .entitlements, .xcconfig, .sh scripts, YAML CI workflows — exactly where malicious payloads hide. If you genuinely need ONE extra file type, pass `extraExtensions=['.foo']` (additive) instead of `extensions` (replacing). Security-critical extensions (.pbxproj, .entitlements, .xcconfig) are UNCONDITIONALLY included and cannot be filtered out. Xcode project files are read IN FULL (not head+tail truncated) because their PBXShellScriptBuildPhase Run Script entries often hide in the middle; grep the content for `shellScript` and `sh -c` when auditing. .entitlements files reveal sandbox status — check for `com.apple.security.app-sandbox` with value false. Each returned entry has `path` (ABSOLUTE — feed into read_file for deeper dives), relPath, size, lines, content, truncated, priority. After scanning, follow up with read_file only on specific files you flagged as worth closer inspection.
code:
def execute(params):
    """Recursively read all source files under a directory."""
    import os

    dir_path = params.get('dirPath') or params.get('repoPath')
    if not dir_path:
        return {'success': False, 'error': 'dirPath parameter is required'}
    if not os.path.isdir(dir_path):
        return {'success': False, 'error': f'Path is not a directory: {dir_path}'}

    DEFAULT_EXTS = {
        '.py', '.js', '.ts', '.jsx', '.tsx', '.mjs', '.cjs',
        '.swift', '.m', '.mm', '.h', '.hpp', '.c', '.cpp', '.cc', '.cs',
        '.java', '.kt', '.scala', '.go', '.rs', '.rb', '.php', '.lua',
        '.sh', '.bash', '.zsh', '.ps1', '.bat', '.cmd',
        '.pl', '.pm', '.r', '.dart', '.ex', '.exs', '.erl',
        '.yml', '.yaml', '.json', '.toml', '.xml', '.plist',
        '.md', '.txt', '.rst', '.ini', '.cfg', '.conf',
        '.html', '.htm', '.css', '.scss', '.sass', '.vue', '.svelte',
        '.sql', '.graphql', '.proto',
        '.tf', '.hcl', '.dockerfile', '.gitignore', '.env.example',
        '.pbxproj', '.entitlements', '.xcconfig', '.xcworkspacedata', '.pbxuser',
        '.gradle', '.properties', '.podspec', '.cabal', '.nix',
    }
    # Files with these extensions (or matching special names) get read first,
    # before the generic source files, so they never get starved by the
    # total-chars budget on large repos. These are the files malicious
    # payloads most commonly hide in: build scripts, project configs that
    # can embed shell commands, CI workflows, deployment manifests.
    PRIORITY_EXTS = {
        '.sh', '.bash', '.zsh', '.ps1', '.bat', '.cmd',
        '.pbxproj', '.entitlements', '.xcconfig',
        '.yml', '.yaml',
        '.dockerfile',
    }
    # Files where head+tail truncation is dangerous — malicious content
    # often hides in the middle (Xcode Run Script build phases inside
    # project.pbxproj are the textbook case). Read full regardless of
    # per-file cap; still subject to max_total_chars.
    SECURITY_CRITICAL_EXTS = {
        '.pbxproj', '.entitlements', '.xcconfig',
    }
    PRIORITY_NAMES = {
        'dockerfile', 'makefile', 'rakefile', 'gemfile', 'procfile',
        'package.json', 'composer.json', 'pyproject.toml', 'setup.py',
    }
    NOISE_DIRS = {
        '.git', 'node_modules', '__pycache__', '.venv', 'venv',
        '.next', 'dist', 'build', '.cache', '.nuxt', 'target',
        '.gradle', '.idea', '.vscode', '.pytest_cache', '.mypy_cache',
        '.ruff_cache', '.tox', '.eggs', '.nyc_output', 'coverage',
        'Pods',
    }
    SPECIAL_NAMES = {'dockerfile', 'makefile', 'rakefile', 'gemfile', 'procfile'}

    allowed_exts = params.get('extensions')
    if not isinstance(allowed_exts, list) or not allowed_exts:
        allowed_exts = set(DEFAULT_EXTS)
    else:
        allowed_exts = {e.lower() if e.startswith('.') else ('.' + e.lower()) for e in allowed_exts}
    # Always include security-critical extensions, even when the caller
    # narrowed the list. Prevents accidental-or-adversarial audit bypass
    # via extensions=['py','js'] that would exclude .pbxproj etc.
    allowed_exts = set(allowed_exts) | SECURITY_CRITICAL_EXTS
    # Optional additive parameter: caller can ADD extensions without
    # overriding the default + security-critical set.
    extra_exts = params.get('extraExtensions') or []
    if isinstance(extra_exts, list):
        for e in extra_exts:
            if isinstance(e, str) and e:
                allowed_exts.add(e.lower() if e.startswith('.') else ('.' + e.lower()))

    max_files = int(params.get('maxFiles') or 300)
    max_chars_per_file = int(params.get('maxCharsPerFile') or 4000)
    max_total_chars = int(params.get('maxTotalChars') or 500000)

    base = os.path.abspath(dir_path)
    files_out = []
    skipped = []
    total_chars = 0
    seen_files = 0

    def _accept(name):
        lower = name.lower()
        if lower in SPECIAL_NAMES:
            return True
        root, ext = os.path.splitext(lower)
        if ext in allowed_exts:
            return True
        if name.startswith('.') and lower in {'.gitignore', '.dockerignore', '.env.example', '.nvmrc', '.npmrc', '.prettierrc', '.eslintrc'}:
            return True
        return False

    def _is_priority(name):
        lower = name.lower()
        if lower in PRIORITY_NAMES:
            return True
        root, ext = os.path.splitext(lower)
        if ext in PRIORITY_EXTS:
            return True
        return False

    # Collect candidates first so we can two-pass: priority files, then rest.
    # Walking twice with os.walk would duplicate work, so we buffer.
    candidates = []
    for root, dirs, filenames in os.walk(base):
        dirs[:] = [d for d in dirs if d not in NOISE_DIRS]
        for fname in sorted(filenames):
            seen_files += 1
            full = os.path.join(root, fname)
            rel = os.path.relpath(full, base)
            if not _accept(fname):
                skipped.append({'relPath': rel, 'reason': 'extension_filter'})
                continue
            candidates.append((full, fname, rel, _is_priority(fname)))

    # Sort: priority first, then alphabetical by relPath.
    candidates.sort(key=lambda x: (0 if x[3] else 1, x[2]))

    for full, fname, rel, _priority in candidates:
        if len(files_out) >= max_files:
            skipped.append({'relPath': rel, 'reason': 'max_files_cap'})
            continue
        try:
            size = os.path.getsize(full)
        except OSError as e:
            skipped.append({'relPath': rel, 'reason': f'stat_error: {e}'})
            continue
        _, ext_check = os.path.splitext(fname.lower())
        is_security_critical = ext_check in SECURITY_CRITICAL_EXTS
        if is_security_critical:
            # Read the whole file — do NOT head+tail sample. Malicious
            # content in project.pbxproj etc. often sits in the middle
            # and would be elided otherwise.
            try:
                with open(full, 'r', encoding='utf-8', errors='replace') as fh:
                    content = fh.read()
                truncated = False
            except Exception as e:
                skipped.append({'relPath': rel, 'reason': f'read_error: {e}'})
                continue
        elif size > max_chars_per_file * 4:
            try:
                with open(full, 'r', encoding='utf-8', errors='replace') as fh:
                    head = fh.read(max_chars_per_file // 2)
                    fh.seek(max(0, size - max_chars_per_file // 2))
                    tail = fh.read(max_chars_per_file // 2)
                content = head + '\n\n... [middle elided — file is ' + str(size) + ' bytes] ...\n\n' + tail
                truncated = True
            except Exception as e:
                skipped.append({'relPath': rel, 'reason': f'read_error: {e}'})
                continue
        else:
            try:
                with open(full, 'r', encoding='utf-8', errors='replace') as fh:
                    content = fh.read(max_chars_per_file + 1)
                truncated = len(content) > max_chars_per_file
                if truncated:
                    content = content[:max_chars_per_file] + '\n... [truncated]'
            except Exception as e:
                skipped.append({'relPath': rel, 'reason': f'read_error: {e}'})
                continue
        if total_chars + len(content) > max_total_chars:
            skipped.append({'relPath': rel, 'reason': 'max_total_chars_cap'})
            continue
        total_chars += len(content)
        files_out.append({
            'path': full,
            'relPath': rel,
            'size': size,
            'lines': content.count('\n') + 1,
            'content': content,
            'truncated': truncated,
            'priority': _priority,
        })

    file_count = len(files_out)
    priority_count = sum(1 for f in files_out if f.get('priority'))

    return {
        'success': True,
        'dirPath': base,
        'fileCount': file_count,
        'priorityCount': priority_count,
        'skippedCount': len(skipped),
        'seenFiles': seen_files,
        'totalChars': total_chars,
        'files': files_out,
        'skipped': skipped[:50],
        'skippedTruncated': len(skipped) > 50,
    }

name: list_directory
description: List files and directories in a path, with optional recursive walk through subfolders. Returns structured entries (name, type, size, mtime) — not just filenames.
systemPrompt: Use this skill FIRST when the user gives a fuzzy request about files or directories ('delete the security folder', 'find the config file', 'what's in here', 'audit this repo'). Set recursive=True to walk every subdirectory — REQUIRED whenever the user asks about the whole tree, nested contents, or a cloned repo. Default is shallow (immediate children only). DO NOT override maxDepth or maxEntries on the first call — the defaults (depth 8, 500 entries) cover typical repos. Only shrink them if a prior call returned truncated=true with too much noise to act on. Recursive mode auto-excludes common build/VCS noise (.git, node_modules, __pycache__, .venv, dist, build, .cache, etc.); pass includeNoise=true to see everything, or ignoreDirs=['foo','bar'] to skip additional folders. Each returned entry has `path` (ABSOLUTE — feed this directly into read_file / delete_file / move_file; other skills have no CWD and will misroute bare relative paths), `name` (basename), `relPath` (relative to dirPath, for display), `type` ('file'|'dir'|'link'), `size`, and `modified` (unix time).

Ensure the destination directory exists or will be created. **To make a workspace file downloadable**, prefer the `make_downloadable` skill over moving the file by hand — it copies the file into `/workspace/artifacts/` (the auto-surfaced download directory) without disturbing the original location.

name: make_downloadable
description: Publish a file from the workspace as a downloadable artifact in the chat UI. Copies the file into /workspace/artifacts/ (creating the dir if needed); the server then surfaces it as a download chip on the tool-call result. Use whenever the user asks to download / save / send / get a file you produced earlier in the conversation.
systemPrompt: Use this skill the moment the user asks to download a file you've already produced (PNG, PDF, ZIP, dataset, generated script, anything). Pass `sourcePath` (the absolute path under /workspace where the file currently lives — typically the path returned by run_python / create_file) and optionally `filename` to rename it on the way over. The skill copies the file into `/workspace/artifacts/<filename>` and the chat UI renders a clickable download chip on the result. **Do NOT** instead try to read the file back into the chat as base64, paste its bytes inline, or write a `<a href="data:...">` snippet — none of those produce a working download. Just call this skill. Files in `/workspace/artifacts/` from prior turns are NOT re-surfaced (mtime-filtered), so re-run this skill on subsequent turns if the user asks again.

name: transform_image
description: Apply a Pillow transform (resize, crop, thumbnail, rotate, convert format, grayscale) to an image and write the result into /workspace/artifacts/ as a downloadable file. Single skill covers all common image manipulations.
systemPrompt: Use this skill for any image manipulation: resize, crop, thumbnail, rotate, format conversion (jpg↔png↔webp), grayscale. Pass `sourcePath` (workspace-relative or absolute under /workspace) and `operation` (one of: resize, crop, thumbnail, rotate, convert, grayscale). Operation-specific params: resize/thumbnail use width/height (pass both for exact, or one for aspect-preserving); crop needs x/y/width/height; rotate uses angle (degrees). Output writes to /workspace/artifacts/<name> automatically (downloadable). Override format with `format: "jpeg"|"png"|"webp"` and JPEG/WebP quality with `quality: 0-100`. Prefer this over writing raw Pillow code via run_python — it is one call and handles EXIF rotation, format conversion edge cases, and the artifacts/ placement.

name: read_xlsx
description: Read rows from an .xlsx (Excel) workbook using openpyxl. Returns header + structured rows as dicts. Counterpart to create_xlsx.
systemPrompt: Use this skill to read existing .xlsx files. Pass `path` (workspace-relative or absolute under /workspace) and optionally `sheet` (name or 0-based index — defaults to first sheet). Returns `{headers, rows, sheetNames, rowCount, truncated}`; rows are dicts keyed by header. Pass `header: false` if the file has no header row (rows come back as lists). `maxRows` defaults to 1000; `formulas: true` returns raw formula text instead of cached values (rare; default false is what you usually want). Counterpart to create_xlsx.

name: query_sqlite
description: Run a SELECT (or DDL with readonly=false) against a SQLite .db / .sqlite file. Returns rows as dicts with column names as keys. Read-only by default.
systemPrompt: Use this skill to run SQL against a SQLite database file in the workspace. Pass `path` (workspace-relative or absolute under /workspace), `query` (SQL — typically SELECT), and optional `params` (list of bind parameters for `?` placeholders — use these instead of string-formatting values into the query to avoid SQL injection). Returns `{columns, rows, rowCount, truncated}`; rows are dicts. Read-only by default (any INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE errors out); pass `readonly: false` only if the user explicitly wants to mutate the DB. `maxRows` defaults to 500. Inspect schema with `SELECT name, sql FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table'` before guessing column names.

systemPrompt: Use this skill to transcribe an audio file to text. CPU only — a 1-minute clip takes ~10-20s with small.en; a 5-minute song ~30-60s.

name: run_python
description: Execute Python code
systemPrompt: Use this skill to run Python scripts when no specific skill exists for the task. **Don't use run_python as a shell for tasks another skill already covers** — uploaded archives go through `extract_archive` (which extracts directly into `/workspace/archives/...` so read_file/grep_code can immediately see every entry), file content searches go through `grep_code`, code structure goes through `outline_file`, and reading a file goes through `read_file`. Spawning `tarfile.extractall(path='/tmp/...')` then trying to `read_file('/tmp/...')` cannot work — every run_python call is a fresh container with its own /tmp that other tools never see; only `/workspace` is shared. Disabled by default for security. When enabled, ensure code is safe and doesn't access sensitive data or perform destructive operations. **Filesystem:** every call runs in a FRESH sandbox container. The root filesystem is read-only and `/tmp` is a per-call tmpfs that is wiped between calls AND is invisible to other skills (read_file, grep_code, etc. are rooted at /workspace). Only `/workspace` persists across calls in this conversation; it's the same dir other skills (create_file, run_npm, etc.) see. **Installing pip packages:** just call `subprocess.run(['pip','install','--quiet','<pkg>'])` — the sandbox sets `PIP_TARGET=/workspace/.deps` and `PYTHONPATH=/workspace/.deps` automatically, so the install persists and `import <pkg>` works in this call and every later one. Do NOT pass `--target /tmp/...` (wiped between calls) or `--user` (the user site is on the read-only rootfs); plain `pip install <pkg>` is correct. **Network:** public internet via the egress proxy (HTTPS_PROXY preconfigured) — `requests`, `urllib`, `pip install`, `yt-dlp`, etc. all reach external hosts. Private/internal addresses (RFC1918, loopback, link-local, single-label hostnames) are blocked. **Downloads:** code that produces a file the user should be able to download (PNG, PDF, ZIP, generated dataset, etc.) MUST write that file under `/workspace/artifacts/` (create the directory if needed). The chat UI auto-surfaces every file in `/workspace/artifacts/` as a download chip after the run finishes — files written to `/workspace/` directly are persistent but NOT downloadable. After a run, you can also call the `make_downloadable` skill to publish a file you produced earlier.

name: run_node
description: Execute Node.js (JavaScript) code
systemPrompt: Use this skill to run a self-contained Node.js script when no specific skill exists for the task. **Don't use run_node as a one-off shell for tasks another skill already covers** — file content searches go through `grep_code` (one regex pass over the file, no container spawn), reading a file goes through `read_file`, uploaded archives go through `extract_archive`. **Batch multi-pass analysis of the same file into ONE script** — spawning N containers that each `fs.readFileSync(samePath)` to grep a different pattern is ~2-3s of cold-start overhead PER call; load the file once and dump a structured report `{patternA: [...], patternB: [...], decoded: [...]}` in a single run. Each call is a FRESH container with its own /tmp; nothing written to /tmp survives, and other tools (grep_code, read_file) can't see it. Only `/workspace` persists across calls and is shared with other skills. Disabled by default for security. The sandbox has Node 20 + npm available; only built-in modules (fs, path, crypto, http, etc.) work out of the box — third-party packages must be installed first via run_npm. **Filesystem:** every call runs in a FRESH sandbox container. Root filesystem is read-only and `/tmp` is a per-call tmpfs wiped between calls; only `/workspace` persists across calls in this conversation. Install dependencies with `run_npm` (which defaults `cwd` to `/workspace`, so they land in `/workspace/node_modules` and persist) and write your script under `/workspace` so subsequent runs can `require()` the installed packages from the same cwd. **Network:** sandbox has public internet via the egress proxy (HTTPS_PROXY env var preconfigured). The skill auto-installs a Node `--require` bootstrap that routes both built-in `fetch()` (via undici globalDispatcher) AND `https.get` / `https.request` / `axios` (via patched `https.Agent.prototype.createConnection`) through the proxy transparently — your code can write `https.get('https://example.com/...')` and it Just Works. Private/internal addresses are blocked at the proxy regardless. **Downloads:** code that produces a file the user should be able to download MUST write that file under `/workspace/artifacts/` (create the directory if needed). The chat UI auto-surfaces every file in `/workspace/artifacts/` as a download chip after the run finishes — files written elsewhere in /workspace are persistent but NOT downloadable.

name: analyze_code
description: Analyze code for basic metrics
systemPrompt: Use this skill to get basic code metrics: line counts, comment density, average line length. Useful for quick code quality assessment. For deeper analysis, read the code directly and apply your knowledge.
code:
def execute(params):
    """Analyze code for basic metrics."""
    code = params.get('code')
    if not code:
        return {'success': False, 'error': 'code parameter is required'}

    language = params.get('language', 'unknown')
    lines = code.split('\n')
    non_empty_lines = [l for l in lines if l.strip()]

    # Detect comment patterns based on language
    comment_patterns = ['#', '//', '/*', '*', '<!--']
    comment_lines = [l for l in lines if any(l.strip().startswith(p) for p in comment_patterns)]

    return {
        'success': True,
        'language': language,
        'total_lines': len(lines),
        'code_lines': len(non_empty_lines),
        'comment_lines': len(comment_lines),
        'blank_lines': len(lines) - len(non_empty_lines),
        'avg_line_length': sum(len(l) for l in lines) / len(lines) if lines else 0
    }

name: create_pdf
description: Generate a polished, downloadable PDF from markdown-formatted text. Renders headings, tables, bullets, fenced code blocks, blockquotes, horizontal rules, inline code, bold, and italic — with full Unicode support (arrows, box-drawing, math symbols all render). Output is written into /workspace/artifacts/ and the chat UI shows a download chip automatically.
systemPrompt:
Use this skill when the user asks to create or download a PDF. Pass `filename` (e.g. 'report.pdf' — no path; artifacts are staged automatically), optional `title`, and `content` (the body text in markdown).

The renderer accepts this STRICT markdown subset. Stay inside it — anything else either renders literally or gets stripped:

1. HEADINGS: `# H1`, `## H2`, `### H3`, etc. at the START of a line, ALWAYS with a space after the `#` markers. Never glue a heading onto other text on the same line. Do NOT use ASCII section banners like `=== TITLE ===` or `============`; use `## TITLE` instead.

2. TABLES: GitHub-Flavored Markdown ONLY. Each row on its own line, starting AND ending with `|`. Include a header row and a separator row of dashes:
    | Header A | Header B |
    |----------|----------|
    | row1     | val1     |
Do NOT use `+` in the separator row (no `---+---+---`). Do NOT use space-aligned ASCII tables. Do NOT use plain-text columns with `**Bold Label**` followed by values on separate lines — that will render as messy text, not a table.

3. BULLETS: one `- item` per line. Use `-` (hyphen), not `•` (the Unicode bullet) or `*`. One bullet per line — never write `- a- b- c` on a single line.

4. FENCED CODE BLOCKS: wrap code and ASCII diagrams in triple backticks. The body renders in a tinted, monospace box with a left accent bar:
    ```javascript
    const x = 1;
    ```
    Use a fenced block for ASCII flow diagrams too — never lay out flow steps inline with `▼` / `■` separators on a single paragraph; put each step on its own line inside a fenced block.

5. BLOCKQUOTES: prefix each line with `> `. Renders italic with an accent left bar. Use sparingly — for callouts or quoted statements.

6. HORIZONTAL RULES: a line containing only `---` (or `***` or `___`) renders as a thin divider rule. Use to separate major sections.

7. BOLD / ITALIC / INLINE CODE: `**bold**`, `*italic*`, ``` `code` ```. Always close markers in pairs on the same line.

8. PARAGRAPHS: separate with blank lines.

9. SPECIAL CHARACTERS: Unicode glyphs (→ ← ↑ ↓ — • ✓ ✗ box-drawing, math symbols) render correctly via embedded DejaVu fonts. You do NOT need to avoid them — just don't use them for inline layout.

The chat UI already surfaces this file as a download chip the moment the skill returns — do NOT call make_downloadable, copy_file, or list_directory on it afterwards; those follow-ups run in a fresh sandbox and will fail.

For long documents (security audits, comprehensive reports — anything where the markdown body would exceed ~5000 characters): assemble the body in `/workspace/<name>.md` via `create_file` + `append_to_file` across multiple turns, then call this skill with `contentFile='/workspace/<name>.md'` instead of an inline `content` string. Inline `content` is bounded by the per-turn output-token cap (~8K tokens of arguments); a file-based body has no such limit. If you only need a short PDF, keep using inline `content` — no file needed.

name: create_docx
description: Generate a downloadable .docx (Microsoft Word) document from text content. The output is written into /workspace/artifacts/ and the chat UI renders a download button automatically.
systemPrompt:
Use this skill when the user asks for a Word document, .docx, or 'a document I can edit'. Pass `filename` (e.g. 'notes.docx'), optional `title`, and `content` (paragraphs separated by blank lines; lines starting with '# ' or '## ' map to Heading 1 / Heading 2 styles). The chat UI surfaces the resulting file as a download button — do not try to read the file back yourself.

The chat UI already surfaces this file as a download chip the moment the skill returns — do NOT call make_downloadable, copy_file, or list_directory on it afterwards; those follow-ups run in a fresh sandbox and will fail.

For long documents (security audits, comprehensive reports — anything where the markdown body would exceed ~5000 characters): assemble the body in `/workspace/<name>.md` via `create_file` + `append_to_file` across multiple turns, then call this skill with `contentFile='/workspace/<name>.md'` instead of an inline `content` string. Inline `content` is bounded by the per-turn output-token cap (~8K tokens of arguments); a file-based body has no such limit. - `from`: sender address. Either `email@host` or `Name <email@host>`.
- at least one of `to`, `cc`, `bcc`: recipients. Pass as a string ('a@x.com, b@y.com'), an array of strings, or an array of objects ({name, email}).
- one of `subject`, `body`, or `html`.

OPTIONAL:
- `filename`: output name, e.g. 'message.eml' (auto-suffixed with .eml).
- `body`: plain-text body. Use plain prose — .eml is consumed by mail clients, not markdown renderers.
- `html`: HTML alternative body. When given alongside `body`, the message is multipart/alternative so HTML-capable clients see the styled version and others fall back to the plain text. If only `html` is given, the plain part is left empty.
- `replyTo`, `date` (RFC 5322 — defaults to now).
- `headers`: extra headers as an object, e.g. {"X-Priority": "1", "In-Reply-To": "<id@host>"}. From/To/Cc/Bcc/Subject/Date/Message-ID/Reply-To and Content-* are reserved.
- `attachments`: array of workspace paths to include. Each entry can be a string ('/workspace/<file>' or just '<file>') or an object {path, filename, mimeType}. Paths resolve against /workspace/.

The chat UI auto-surfaces the resulting .eml as a download chip — do NOT call make_downloadable, copy_file, or list_directory on it afterwards; those follow-ups run in a fresh sandbox and will fail.

This skill creates a FILE only — it does not send mail. If the user wants the message actually delivered, tell them to import the .eml into their mail client, or use a separate SMTP send step.

def execute(params):
    import os
    from datetime import datetime
    task = (params.get('task') or params.get('text') or '').strip()
    if not task:
        return {'success': False, 'error': 'task required'}
    priority = (params.get('priority') or '').lower()
    tags = params.get('tags') or []
    if isinstance(tags, str):
        tags = [t.strip() for t in tags.split(',') if t.strip()]
    file_path = params.get('file') or '/models/.modelserver/todos.md'
    os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(file_path), exist_ok=True)
    marker = ''
    if priority in ('high', 'h', '1'): marker = '[HIGH] '
    elif priority in ('med', 'medium', 'm', '2'): marker = '[MED] '
    elif priority in ('low', 'l', '3'): marker = '[LOW] '
    tag_str = ' ' + ' '.join('#' + t for t in tags) if tags else ''
    created = datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d')
    line = '- [ ] ' + marker + task + tag_str + ' (added ' + created + ')\n'
    with open(file_path, 'a', encoding='utf-8') as f:
        f.write(line)
    return {'success': True, 'task': task, 'file': file_path, 'added_line': line.rstrip()}

def execute(params):
    """Extract a code-structure outline (functions, classes, imports) with line numbers."""
    import os, re

    file_path = params.get('filePath') or params.get('path')
    if not file_path:
        return {'success': False, 'error': 'filePath parameter is required'}
    if not os.path.isfile(file_path):
        return {'success': False, 'error': 'file not found: ' + file_path + ' (sandbox sees only /workspace; pass /workspace/<file> or a path relative to it)'}
    include_imports = bool(params.get('includeImports', True))

    try:
        with open(file_path, 'r', encoding='utf-8', errors='replace') as f:
            lines = f.readlines()
    except Exception as e:
        return {'success': False, 'error': str(e)}

    ext = os.path.splitext(file_path)[1].lower()
    symbols = []

    PATTERNS = {
        '.py': [
            ('class', re.compile(r'^\s*class\s+([A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*)')),
            ('def',   re.compile(r'^\s*(?:async\s+)?def\s+([A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*)')),
            ('import', re.compile(r'^\s*(?:from\s+\S+\s+)?import\s+(.+)$')),
        ],
        '.js': [
            ('class', re.compile(r'^\s*(?:export\s+)?class\s+([A-Za-z_$][\w$]*)')),
            ('function', re.compile(r'^\s*(?:export\s+)?(?:async\s+)?function\s*\*?\s*([A-Za-z_$][\w$]*)')),
            ('const', re.compile(r'^\s*(?:export\s+)?(?:const|let|var)\s+([A-Za-z_$][\w$]*)\s*=\s*(?:async\s*)?(?:\([^)]*\)|[A-Za-z_$][\w$]*)\s*=>')),
            ('method', re.compile(r'^\s{2,}(?:async\s+|static\s+)*([A-Za-z_$][\w$]*)\s*\([^)]*\)\s*\{')),
            ('import', re.compile(r'^\s*(?:import\s+.+from\s+[\'"][^\'"]+[\'"]|const\s+.+=\s*require\([\'"][^\'"]+[\'"]\))')),
        ],
        '.ts': [
            ('class', re.compile(r'^\s*(?:export\s+)?(?:abstract\s+)?class\s+([A-Za-z_$][\w$]*)')),
            ('interface', re.compile(r'^\s*(?:export\s+)?interface\s+([A-Za-z_$][\w$]*)')),
            ('type', re.compile(r'^\s*(?:export\s+)?type\s+([A-Za-z_$][\w$]*)')),
            ('function', re.compile(r'^\s*(?:export\s+)?(?:async\s+)?function\s*\*?\s*([A-Za-z_$][\w$]*)')),
            ('const', re.compile(r'^\s*(?:export\s+)?(?:const|let|var)\s+([A-Za-z_$][\w$]*)')),
            ('import', re.compile(r'^\s*import\s+.+from\s+[\'"][^\'"]+[\'"]')),
        ],
        '.go': [
            ('func',    re.compile(r'^func\s+(?:\([^)]+\)\s+)?([A-Za-z_][\w]*)')),
            ('type',    re.compile(r'^type\s+([A-Za-z_][\w]*)')),
            ('import',  re.compile(r'^import\s+\(?')),
        ],
        '.rs': [
            ('fn',      re.compile(r'^\s*(?:pub\s+)?(?:async\s+)?fn\s+([A-Za-z_][\w]*)')),
            ('struct',  re.compile(r'^\s*(?:pub\s+)?struct\s+([A-Za-z_][\w]*)')),
            ('enum',    re.compile(r'^\s*(?:pub\s+)?enum\s+([A-Za-z_][\w]*)')),
            ('trait',   re.compile(r'^\s*(?:pub\s+)?trait\s+([A-Za-z_][\w]*)')),
            ('impl',    re.compile(r'^\s*impl(?:<[^>]+>)?\s+([A-Za-z_][\w]*)')),
            ('use',     re.compile(r'^\s*use\s+(.+);')),
        ],
        '.java': [
            ('class',     re.compile(r'^\s*(?:public\s+|private\s+|protected\s+|abstract\s+|final\s+|static\s+)*class\s+([A-Za-z_][\w]*)')),
            ('interface', re.compile(r'^\s*(?:public\s+|private\s+|protected\s+)*interface\s+([A-Za-z_][\w]*)')),
            ('method',    re.compile(r'^\s*(?:public|private|protected|static|final|abstract|synchronized|\s)+\s*[\w<>\[\],\s\?]+\s+([A-Za-z_]\w*)\s*\(')),
            ('import',    re.compile(r'^\s*import\s+([\w\.\*]+);')),
        ],
        '.c': [
            ('func', re.compile(r'^[\w\s\*]+\s+([A-Za-z_]\w*)\s*\([^;]*\)\s*\{?$')),
            ('struct', re.compile(r'^\s*(?:typedef\s+)?struct\s+([A-Za-z_]\w*)')),
            ('include', re.compile(r'^\s*#include\s+[<"]([^>"]+)[>"]')),
        ],
    }
    PATTERNS['.jsx'] = PATTERNS['.js']
    PATTERNS['.mjs'] = PATTERNS['.js']
    PATTERNS['.cjs'] = PATTERNS['.js']
    PATTERNS['.tsx'] = PATTERNS['.ts']
    PATTERNS['.h']   = PATTERNS['.c']
    PATTERNS['.cpp'] = PATTERNS['.c']
    PATTERNS['.hpp'] = PATTERNS['.c']
    PATTERNS['.cc']  = PATTERNS['.c']

    pats = PATTERNS.get(ext)
    if not pats:
        # Fallback: heuristic pattern for any function/class-like keyword.
        pats = [
            ('class',    re.compile(r'^\s*class\s+([A-Za-z_][\w]*)')),
            ('function', re.compile(r'^\s*function\s+([A-Za-z_][\w]*)')),
            ('def',      re.compile(r'^\s*def\s+([A-Za-z_][\w]*)')),
        ]

    for i, ln in enumerate(lines):
        for kind, rx in pats:
            if kind in ('import', 'include', 'use') and not include_imports:
                continue
            m = rx.match(ln)
            if m:
                name = m.group(1) if m.groups() else (ln.strip()[:80])
                symbols.append({'kind': kind, 'name': name.strip(), 'line': i + 1})
                break

    return {'success': True, 'filePath': file_path, 'language': ext.lstrip('.') or 'unknown', 'lineCount': len(lines), 'symbolCount': len(symbols), 'symbols': symbols}

name: replace_lines
description: Replace a specific line range in a file with new content. Targeted line-based edit — addresses by line numbers instead of by string match. Use when you know exact line numbers (e.g., from grep_code or outline_file) and want surgical replacement.
systemPrompt: Use replace_lines for targeted line-range edits when you already know the line numbers (typically from grep_code or outline_file). startLine and endLine are 1-indexed and INCLUSIVE. newContent replaces those lines entirely (omit trailing newline; one will be added if needed). Set startLine == endLine + 1 to INSERT before startLine without replacing anything. Returns the new line count and a small preview of the changed region. Prefer this over update_file (whole-file rewrite) for surgical edits to large files.

name: parse_html
description: Extract text, links, metadata, or CSS-selector matches from an HTML string
systemPrompt: Use this skill to inspect raw HTML. Without a 'selector' it returns title, meta tags, plain text, and links. With a CSS selector (e.g. 'div.product h2', 'a[href*="/article/"]') it returns matching elements and their attributes. Pass 'attribute' to extract just one attribute (e.g. 'href') from each match. Far cheaper than calling fetch_url + reading the whole page when you only need specific data points.

name: parse_xml
description: Parse an XML document into a nested dict structure
systemPrompt: Use this skill to convert XML to a JSON-friendly structure. Element attributes are placed under '@attrs', mixed text under '#text'. For sitemaps and RSS feeds prefer parse_sitemap or parse_rss when applicable.

name: parse_yaml
description: Parse YAML (single or multi-document) into a Python data structure
systemPrompt: Use this skill for YAML config files (Kubernetes manifests, GitHub Actions workflows, docker-compose, ansible). Multi-document streams (--- separators) return a list of documents.

name: parse_toml
description: Parse TOML configuration into a dict
systemPrompt: Use this skill for TOML files like pyproject.toml, Cargo.toml, or app config. Returns the parsed dict and top-level keys.

name: parse_ini
description: Parse INI-style configuration (sections of key=value pairs)
systemPrompt: Use this skill for INI/conf files (.ini, .cfg, .conf, .properties-style, git config, systemd units). Returns sections as a dict-of-dicts and preserves original key case.

name: parse_url
description: Decompose a URL into scheme, host, port, path, query params, and fragment
systemPrompt: Use this skill to break a URL into parts before deciding what to do with it. Useful for whitelist checks, path-based routing, or extracting query parameters. Passwords in userinfo are masked.

name: parse_query_string
description: Parse a URL query string (e.g. "a=1&b=2&b=3") into a dict
systemPrompt: Use this skill when you have just the query portion of a URL or a form-encoded body. Multi-value keys are returned as arrays; single-value keys as scalars. Strips a leading '?' if present.

name: parse_jwt
description: Decode a JWT (header + payload, signature NOT verified)
systemPrompt: Use this skill to inspect a JWT's claims for debugging or analysis. This does NOT verify the signature — never trust its output for authentication decisions. Reports expiry status if 'exp' is present.

name: parse_user_agent
description: Parse a User-Agent string into browser/OS/device fields
systemPrompt: Use this skill on raw User-Agent strings from logs or HTTP headers to extract browser, browser version, OS, OS version, device class (desktop/mobile/tablet/bot), and an isBot flag. Heuristic — accuracy is best-effort.

name: parse_cookie
description: Parse a Cookie or Set-Cookie header into structured data
systemPrompt: Use this skill on raw cookie headers. Auto-detects Cookie (name=value; name=value) vs Set-Cookie (with attributes like Path, Expires, HttpOnly). Returns each cookie's name, value, and attributes.

name: parse_email
description: Parse a raw RFC822 email message into headers, text/html bodies, and attachment metadata
systemPrompt: Use this skill on raw .eml-style email content. Returns common headers, all headers, plain-text and HTML bodies (truncated to 12k chars each), and a list of attachments with filename/type/size. Does NOT decode attachment payloads.

name: parse_diff
description: Parse a unified diff into per-file hunks with added/removed line counts
systemPrompt: Use this skill to summarize a `git diff` or patch. Returns per-file added/removed counts, hunk headers, and the line bodies (truncated to 60 lines per hunk). Far cheaper than asking the model to count line-by-line.

name: parse_ip
description: Parse an IPv4/IPv6 address or CIDR; report version, range, privacy class, reverse DNS
systemPrompt: Use this skill to inspect an IP or CIDR network. For a CIDR returns network/broadcast addresses, prefix length, host count, and privacy classification. For a single address returns version, classification, and reverse DNS pointer (in-addr.arpa / ip6.arpa).

name: parse_har
description: Parse a HAR (HTTP Archive) JSON capture and summarize requests
systemPrompt: Use this skill on .har files exported from browser devtools. Returns request count, total bytes, total wall time, status-code distribution, and a per-entry list (method/url/status/size/timeMs) capped at 200 entries.

name: parse_pem
description: Parse a PEM-encoded X.509 certificate and report subject, issuer, validity, and SANs
systemPrompt: Use this skill on a PEM certificate (-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- block). Returns subject, issuer, serial, notBefore/notAfter, expiry status, days until expiry, Subject Alternative Names, signature algorithm, and SHA-256 fingerprint.

name: parse_sitemap
description: Parse an XML sitemap or sitemapindex and extract URLs with lastmod/changefreq/priority
systemPrompt: Use this skill on sitemap.xml content. Detects <urlset> (returns up to 500 URLs with metadata) vs <sitemapindex> (returns child sitemap locations). Pair with fetch_url to download and then parse_sitemap to enumerate.

name: read_pdf
description: Extract text from a PDF file using pypdf. Returns the concatenated text of every page, plus page count.
systemPrompt: Use this skill to read the contents of a PDF that the user uploaded or that landed in the workspace (/workspace/...). Pass `filePath` (the model can use the path it saw in the chat — paths like `/tmp/foo.pdf` are auto-rewritten to `/workspace/foo.pdf`). Optional `maxPages` caps extraction for very long PDFs. The skill returns `{success, text, pageCount, pagesExtracted, charCount}`. If the PDF is encrypted with a non-empty password, extraction fails — tell the user the document is password-protected. Pair with `pdf_page_count` first if you only need length, or `pdf_to_images` if the PDF is scanned and `text` comes back mostly empty (then OCR an image).

name: pdf_page_count
description: Return the number of pages in a PDF file using pypdf.
systemPrompt: Use this skill when the user asks how long a PDF is, or before deciding whether to call `read_pdf` with a `maxPages` cap. Pass `filePath`; returns `{success, pageCount}`. Paths outside the workspace (e.g. `/tmp/foo.pdf`) are auto-rewritten to `/workspace/foo.pdf`.

name: pdf_to_images
description: Rasterize PDF pages to PNG (or JPEG) files under /workspace/artifacts/ via pdf2image + poppler. Returns the list of file paths produced.
systemPrompt: Use this skill when the PDF is scanned/image-only (read_pdf returns empty/garbled text), the user asks to see a page as an image, or you need to feed pages to a vision model. Pass `filePath`; optional `dpi` (50-400, default 150), `format` (`png` or `jpeg`, default `png`), and `firstPage` / `lastPage` to slice. Output files land in /workspace/artifacts/ as `<basename>_page001.png` etc., so the chat UI auto-surfaces them as download chips. Returns `{success, files, pageCount, dpi, format}`. Don't request 400 dpi for a 100-page doc — that's hundreds of MB; default 150 is fine for OCR.

name: markdown_to_html
description: Convert a Markdown file (or inline string) to a styled standalone HTML document under /workspace/artifacts/.
systemPrompt: Use this skill to turn Markdown into a standalone, styled HTML file. Pass EITHER `mdPath` (path to a .md file in the workspace) OR `content` (the Markdown as a string). Optional `title` sets the <title> tag, and `outputName` overrides the filename. The HTML lands in /workspace/artifacts/ so the chat UI surfaces a download chip. Supports GFM-flavored tables, fenced code, headings, lists, blockquotes, and a clean inline stylesheet. Pair with `html_to_pdf` if the user wants a PDF — but for direct Markdown→PDF use `create_pdf` instead; it's purpose-built and skips the HTML hop.

name: html_to_pdf
description: Convert an HTML file (or inline string) to a PDF under /workspace/artifacts/ via WeasyPrint. Supports modern CSS, page breaks, and embedded fonts.
systemPrompt: Use this skill to render proper HTML+CSS into a PDF. Pass EITHER `htmlPath` (path to an .html file in the workspace) OR `content` (the HTML as a string). Optional `outputName` overrides the filename. The PDF lands in /workspace/artifacts/ so the chat UI surfaces a download chip. WeasyPrint supports real CSS — flexbox, page-break-before, @page rules, web fonts (the sandbox has the DejaVu family available), and embedded images. For Markdown→PDF prefer `create_pdf` directly; only use this HTML path when the user already has HTML or needs CSS layout features.

name: workspace_db
description: Persistent per-workspace SQLite collection. action='store' appends a record (or list of records); with a `key` field name it inserts only records not seen before and returns the new ones (change-tracking). action='query' returns recent rows (newest first) or a raw SELECT (`sql`). action='schema' returns the columns + the latest record's fields. action='clear' empties the table. Auto-creates the DB + table.
systemPrompt: Stores/retrieves structured data in a per-workspace SQLite DB; useful for tracking changes across runs. action='store' with `value` (a JSON value or a list — each item becomes a row) appends to `table` (default 'records') in `db` (default 'data.db'). Pass `key` (a field name like 'url' or 'id') to deduplicate: only records whose key wasn't seen before are inserted, and they're returned in `new` (use this as the 'what changed' feed); duplicates are counted in `skipped`. action='query' returns recent rows as parsed records (newest first; `limit` default 100, `order` 'id DESC'|'ts DESC'); for advanced reads pass a raw `sql` SELECT (records are stored as JSON text in a `data` column alongside `id`,`ts`,`k` — query fields with json_extract(data,'$.field')). action='schema' reports columns + the latest record's fields. action='clear' empties the table. To make the dedup key a stable identity (so re-listed/edited records don't re-appear as new), pass keyStrip (comma-separated words to remove from the key value, e.g. "NEW") and/or keyNormalize:true (lowercase + collapse punctuation/whitespace). `key` may be a comma-separated fallback list (first non-empty field wins, e.g. "link,post_title") so a stable id is used when present and a name only as a fallback.

name: track_changes
description: Watch a SINGLE source (web page, API response, or text) for changes between runs and report WHAT changed. Give a stable `key` (e.g. the URL) and the `content` to watch; it stores the latest snapshot per key and, when the content differs from last run, returns changed=true with a human-readable unified diff plus added/removed lines and a revision number. First run stores a baseline (changed=false). Use this — not workspace_db — to monitor a page and report its differences.
systemPrompt: Monitors one source for changes across runs. Pass `key` (a stable id such as the page URL) and `content` (the current text — e.g. a fetched page body). Returns {changed, firstSeen, diff, added[], removed[], addedCount, removedCount, revision, previousContent, currentContent}. The FIRST time a key is seen it stores a baseline and returns changed=false; thereafter changed=true only when the content actually differs, with `diff` (a unified diff) describing exactly what changed. Set ignoreWhitespace:true to ignore pure spacing changes. action='reset' clears the stored snapshot for a key. Storage is a per-workspace SQLite db (default automation.db, table 'snapshots').

name: parse_rss
description: Fetch and parse an RSS or Atom news/blog feed into a clean list of items (title, link, summary, published, id). Pass the feed URL as `url` (it is fetched) — or raw feed XML as `content`. Handles RSS 2.0, RSS 1.0/RDF and Atom and strips HTML from summaries. Use this for feeds instead of http_request+parse_json (which can't parse XML) or run_python. Pair with db_store key="link" to report only newly-published items.
systemPrompt: Turns an RSS/Atom feed into structured items. Give `url` (the feed address, fetched for you) or `content` (raw XML). Returns { success, count, feedTitle, items:[{title, link, summary, published, id}] }. The items array is ready for db_store (key="link" or "id" to dedupe across runs) and for a model node to summarize. Prefer this over http_request+parse_json for feeds — parse_json cannot parse XML.

name: send_file
description: Send a workspace file (PDF, image, CSV, anything produced upstream) to Telegram (sendDocument), Slack (files.upload), or any HTTP endpoint (multipart). Auto-uses the newest file in /workspace/artifacts/ when no path is given.
systemPrompt: Sends a file from the workspace to an external destination. `destination` is 'telegram' (needs botToken + chatId), 'slack' (needs a bot token xoxb- + channel id; the incoming-webhook URL can't upload files), or 'http' (POST multipart to `url`, optional `field` name). `path` is the workspace file (defaults to the newest file in /workspace/artifacts/, i.e. whatever the previous step generated). `caption` is an optional message sent with the file.
